paragraphs.body: A Doughboy: An Odd Object Essay

A parent is someone who protects. They build you up when you’re low. They take care of you through life. A parent teaches you things you don’t know. They mold you into the shape they think is best. They take care of you when you are sick, giving you soup so you can feel better. They comfort you when you’re sad to make the pain go away. They have your back through hardships that you can’t handle yourself. Should a parent know what’s best for you? A parent sacrifices for their child time and time again. Not all parents are the same. A parent goes out of the way to belittle you and make you as small as possible. They make things difficult to understand. They don’t treat you equally even if you’re an adult. A parent is shallow and only cares for themselves. They watch when you’re belittled and add to the fire. They make choices for you always knowing what is right. They judge you for who you are because there’s no way that can’t. They find your insecurities and make sure to make jokes. A parent kneads you into what you believe is right even if it is wrong.

annotations.text: A parent kneads you into what you believe is right even if it is wrong.
annotations.comment: I see the metaphor happening, here, but I'm not quite sure what you mean?

paragraphs.body: When I was younger, I read many story books. Where the characters developed and shined. I’d like to be like a lot of those characters. How happy they looked made me happy. Even if their story had a rough start, they always got their happily ever after. I envy those endings. How life isn’t as simple as stories. How some of the issues in the stories would go a lot differently. Or how they are exactly the same. One of my favorite story books is The Gingerbread Man. How fluid it was since the boy didn’t even have to be gingerbread. A dough boy you can’t catch. The old woman decided to one day bake some bread. She went to her kitchen and began making the dough. After preheating the oven, she mixed all her ingredients together making sure everything was just right. From leveling the flour to sifting the sugar. She shaped the dough into the shape of a little person thinking it would be cute. Just as she was about to put it in the oven it got up and ran away. The little dough boy ran and ran saying “you can’t catch me I’m the doughboy and no one can tell me who I am!”. That he did and he ran straight out of town trying to figure out who he is with nothing being found. He traveled all over trying not to be eaten. Running away from all sorts of people and creatures. Though no one could prepare him for this last creature he met. He got to a river with no way around and there was a fox swimming around. The fox said, “get on my back I’ll take you across”. The doughboy was hesitant to listen to the fox since they were conniving and sneaky. Even so he got on his back and as they went the water got deeper. The fox them said, “get on my nose so you don’t get wet” and so the doughboy listened. He got on the fox’s nose trying to balance. The fox opened his mouth wide and as the doughboy fell, he swallowed him whole. I close my book by and shake my head at this tale. I often think about it and how I and the doughboy in the story are the same. In a constant war of who I am. It makes me sad.

Even if their story had a rough start, they always got their happily ever after. I envy those endings. How life isn’t as simple as stories.

paragraphs.body: Sometimes I think about the old woman in that story and how she makes me feel. How to me she’s not an old woman but one in her thirties. One that grew up with me and maybe still haven’t quite grown up. How she makes me feel sometimes and how I’m not surprised the doughboy ran away. How I felt like yeast that couldn’t rise. A shower of cold water on my skin of bickering and banter. How tired I felt and the pain that was quite numb. No matter how good I was it was never good enough. My shortcomings would always be brought up. Sometimes I just want to be alone playing my video games with a slice of cake.

annotations.comment: I'm _really_interested, here. Let's talk about how you might leverage the metaphor of the dough boy to get more concrete and specific about the connections you're making. :)

paragraphs.body: I remember the smell of the bakery. It was a very small bakery in downtown Pittsburgh. Crisp air that filled the building. The taste of the sweets filling your mouth with not one even touching your lips. Then there were baskets of bread. Rigid could give you a splinter. The bread itself is perfect. The asymmetry and the way the crust caves in on itself cracking. The fluffiness on my tongue as I ate it and the doubts in my mind that followed that I could never make something so good. The joy I felt as I dipped it in a side of cream and chicken soup. The swirling of my spoon wishing it would not end. The comfort I felt while eating it and the misery I felt recreating it in my own kitchen. The stickiness of my hands as I try to get the dough to a perfect consistency. The powdery feeling of the flour as I spread it all over my mother’s glass table in the dining room. The satisfaction of putting the loaf in the oven feeling the heat radiating off. Watching fingers wrinkle as I was the bowls I used, and the sponge get nasty as leftover dough sticks to it like glue. The grip I have on the broom as I sweep leftover flour off the floor. When I finally opened the oven to let my bread loaf rest the smell engulfing my whole house. Fresh bread is not perfect but something special because it was mine. I’ll always remember it as mine.

paragraphs.body: It is hard to really say I ever had much that was mine before I started working. Sure, I had my own room and own things, but it was things my parents paid for, and it was always a luxury. Something they could always hand over my head. When I started working it was like a weight had been lifted off me. I always had stuff and things they couldn’t take because the power was lost. I felt like I belonged in that family our last names didn’t match and sure there was love but it didn’t always seem genuine. If I had left it didn’t seem to be abnormal to anyone. My mother doesn’t want me to grow up, but I must. Eventually growing up is also growing out of people. I don’t hate my family nor have ill will. I couldn’t imagine my life differently, I just wish it was. I don’t want to be a loaf of bread that hasn’t risen long enough. Once that dough is baked there is no going back. Everything you did sticks and the memories flow as a constant reminder of what could have been. With bread you can just try again. Remaking the dough is one task. You cannot do that as a human. Sure, you can make another but the first will still be there and be hurt.
